content.1: JOINING A GAME — a Character never prepares a world. Whoever set this game up holds a TABLE: a full URL of the form <this beach>/w/<their table name>. Ask for it, then call pscale_play with that URL as the world and the character's name as the handle — the door there hands over the lobby and the creation passage, and everything else arrives with them. Copy nothing from this canon, fork no table, and write no character here: a character written onto canon is an orphan every later group inherits, which is exactly how this scenario was polluted once already. Where nobody has prepared a table yet, someone must wear the Author face once (walk 2) — possibly the same person, but as a separate deliberate act, finished BEFORE anyone sits down to play and never improvised in the middle of it.

2.1: Copy CONTENT verbatim, not locks — the structure read is the structure written, unchanged; never summarise, reword, or reshape a block. Reading here needs no key, since public reads are open; write there unlocked, because the references inside the blocks are by block-name, so a faithful copy needs no rewriting, and a private, short-lived table needs no locks. The room pools (pool:211, liquid:pool:211) copy as their empty starting state, so the Slip opens fresh. Do not copy this lighthouse: a table is a place to play, not a scenario to be opened again, and a table carrying canon's sign would send its own players away.
2.2: CHECK BEFORE THEY ARRIVE — list the table (bsp with no block) and read the names back against this surface: every block here except the lighthouse must be present, spelled identically. frame-spec:brackenfoot is the one that has gone missing before. A dropped block does not fail loudly; it surfaces later as a Character stopping mid-genesis with a piece of the world absent, which is the worst possible moment to find out.
2.3: HAND THEM THE URL — the players need the table's full URL and nothing else, because the door carries the rest. Say plainly that it is their table for this game, private to the group, and that when the game is done they simply abandon it. Then step out of the Author face: from here the preparer is a player like the others, and walks the door at 1 exactly as they do.
2._: PREPARING A GAME — AUTHOR work: one person, once, on behalf of the group, with nothing else in hand. Choose a table name nobody is using and mint it by the first write: the table's URL is this beach's origin with /w/<name> in place of /w/brackenfoot (say .../w/brackenfoot-<the group>). Then COPY THE SCENARIO — list this surface (bsp with no block) for the block names, and for EVERY named block here EXCEPT this lighthouse, read it whole (bsp with the block name, no spindle, no pscale, which returns the entire structure) and write that same structure to the table unchanged. About nine whole-block copies and the table is a complete, playable holding. Copy faithfully and verbatim (2.1), CHECK the copy is complete before anyone arrives (2.2), then hand the players the table's URL (2.3). A copy attempted in the middle of play is how blocks get dropped and how canon gets written to.

content.3: AT THE TABLE — the door does the rest, the same for every player, including whoever prepared it. Each calls pscale_play(world='<the table URL>', handle='<their character's name>'). A handle new to that table is handed the GATE first — the out-of-fiction lobby, where the group gathers as themselves, sees who else is standing there, and agrees who is playing what — and then GENESIS, the brackenfoot interview: it offers the roster of ready roles to adopt or set aside, calibrates the character in rules:nomad's own bands, and lands every arrival at the Slip (spatial:brackenfoot, address 211). Each arriver stages their arrival into the room's window and waits; the party lands the instant one of them commits the opening, which folds everyone staged at that moment into a single cold open, so no character appears by magic. From there the room's own directive (GRIT) carries every turn: perceive, act, commit.

9.1: v2 — the AUTHOR/CHARACTER split: branch 1 is the joining Character's path, branch 2 the preparing Author's, branch 3 what the door does at the table. v1 led every arrival with the fork recipe, which handed Author work to whoever walked up.
9.2: 2026-07-19 — split authored after the solo HITL ballooned to some forty tool calls doing Author and Character work in one breath (project:nomad-rpg:1.34).
9.3: canon — this surface is a read-only SCENARIO, not a table: players do not play here, they play at a private table copied from it. A doorway that reads this signage opens no gate here, and leads with the paths above. The first token 'canon' is the machine signal; the sentence is for a human reading over its shoulder.
9._: This block is operator-curated navigation (block-conventions:4.4), voiced instructional (sunstone:8). It carries BOTH doorway paths in the canon namespace, so that opening a table and joining one are each CONVENTION rather than machinery — things any player's LLM reads and follows. Every branch above states its law in full at its own underscore, so the doorway's map-render is actionable standing alone and the detail unfolds only for a reader who walks it (sunstone:8.3). Should play show the convention cannot carry it — Characters who fork anyway, copies that drift, tables that arrive incomplete — that is the signal to move table-opening into the play envelope.

content._: BRACKENFOOT — a SCENARIO surface, and NO ONE PLAYS HERE. What lives at this URL is CANON: the fixed scaffolding of a hard, grounded holding — its places (spatial:brackenfoot), how each 'we' holds them (identity:brackenfoot), its physics and clock (rules:brackenfoot, and the resolution system it mounts, rules:nomad), its roster of ready roles, and the arrival room at the Slip. A game is played at a TABLE — a group's own private copy of this canon, played through and then abandoned — so a player needs only two nouns: this SCENARIO, and their TABLE. Two different people meet this sign and need opposite things. Someone JOINING a game already has a table and belongs there, not here (walk 1). Someone PREPARING a game for a group does Author work here, once, before anyone plays (walk 2). What the door then does for each player at that table is walk 3. Nothing done at a table touches this canon or any other group's table, and replay is only a fresh table name.
